transcript.transcript: All right. And we're back. This is Dr. Fiona Vernal from the University of Connecticut. It is July 24th, 2023. And I am here with Bishop Selders, who's agreed to conduct an oral history. Bishop, if you could give me your full introduction and give me permission to do this recording. Sure. I am John Selders. I am, yeah, there's a couple of titles with that, but I am the pastor of Amistad United Church of Christ here in Hartford, Connecticut. And yes, I give permission for this distinguished colleague and professor to video me and this conversation. All right, thank you. Let's jump right in. Can you start by telling me a little bit about your childhood and your family history and what you understand as far back as your lineage goes. Sure. I am third generation classically trained Pentecostal preacher. I grew up in the denomination, the largest of the Pentecostal denominations, the Church of God in Christ. My grandfather was a progenitor in one of the splintered sects of that tradition. And I come to church work, I often describe it as the, you know, I have a family business like Guido and them, you know. So all I know is church. My grandfather, my paternal grandfather was the Bishop William Earl Selders, Sr. And my paternal grandmother was Mother Annie Easter Selders. And she and he served in St. Louis, the greatest St. Louis area. And my grandfather's work as a bishop and an episcopate in that tradition went through Missouri, Arkansas, Tennessee, and Nebraska. And where were you born? I was born in St. Louis. I went directly from the hospital where my mother birthed me to 5601 McHenry at the corner of McHenry and Hugo in a town called Kenloch, Missouri. It is the first African American town carved out in the state of Missouri that was charted as an African American town. If you've ever flown into St. Louis, into Lambert International Airport, you fly into what was then called back in the day, Kenlock Park. And it is the town for which I was raised in my former years, my formative years. Let's spell that for the AI robot that's gonna transcribe this. K-K-I-N-L-O-C-H, Kenlock. All right, I would have never gotten that one. That's all right. It is the town, you don't know it, but you do know the town that is just east of Kimbuk, Ferguson. I see. So there is a claim to fame. And of course I was very involved in the rebellion that took place right after Mike Brown's killing by Darren Wilson, police officer in that town. So I have lots of history with that town. And of course, with ministry in that region and that St. Louis, but also the near North County, which is where many of the black people lived and still do live. And in terms of your formative years as a young person, so you have your two generations of family history and legacy in the church, What else informed your upbringing as a young man? Sure. So, let me continue on this storytelling. My family migrated from the South. And so, all of my both paternal and maternal roots have their place in what I would call the central region of this country, coming from the Gulf of Mexico through Louisiana. So the soldiers are from a town called, in an area in Louisiana called East Feliciana Parish. In Louisiana, the counties are referred to as parishes. And my mother, that's where my paternal grandparents, my paternal grandfather is from. He was the oldest of 12 children. And never turned back to Louisiana. He went to, we believe Arkansas and then on to St. Louis. My grandmother was his second marriage. His first wife passed and then he caught that, well, my grandmother caught his eye and but he had a child with his first wife. My grandmother also had a child. In fact, too, she had a set of twins with her first husband. He passed. And so there was a little bit of a Brady Bunch that happened in 1924, 1925, when my grandparents married. They both had a child each. They referred, my uncle and aunt referred to each other as sister and brother. And they were eight months apart in age. And how many more Children after marriage to that union? My grandmother and grandfather, there were three boys of which my biological father was the youngest. And I, to the second, that generation that comes after, I'm the only boy child that is legitimately claimed. Let me say all of that, that should give you a hint that there probably are others there were, but they don't get the notice that I did amongst all the girls. And of course, because of that, I also got a lot of attention. I was really trained to be my grandfather's successor in the ministry. That trajectory began probably before I was born. Now I have a similar story of my mother's family. My mother's family comes from Wayne County, Mississippi, which is about literally probably about 100 miles north and east of East Louisiana Parish, Louisiana. So my blood boils when I get down to the Gulf, that region of the country, Mississippi, Louisiana, Alabama, that I can't be there for too long without me. I wake up in a different kind of way because there's some ancestors talking to me and then some ancestral roots there. And all along for probably five generations, there are preachers, in Baptist preachers largely in that trajectory and homeowners, landowners, and my maternal great-grandfather probably was a voter. And we believe as early as the early 1870s, he was registered and did participate in the vote in Mississippi, which would have been a fascinating time because Mississippi elected to the federal government a senator and a congressman to federal office there briefly before the end of reconstruction. So this state that has the largest number of black folk, you know, majority black folk in population in Mississippi, It's quite an interesting conversation and journey, and we still own a little bit of property in that area. So I do know a lot more of that history, but that gives you a sense of who my both my paternal grandfather migrated to the St. Louis area. And my mother was part of family who migrated North and to land in St. Louis. St. Louis was one of the stop off points on that Northern trajectory coming out of that region of the country through St. Louis, Kansas City, Chicago, Indianapolis, Detroit, and Minneapolis. They all landed in those places moving north. In Chicago, Chicago is referred to as up south. That's an inside joke amongst us. What does that mean? Well, of course you get it, but for archival purposes, that up north means we brought everything we did from down South to the North. All of our ways. All of our ways. All of our, both our cultural ways of knowing and being, our spiritual ways and practices, our communal ways of being with one another. All of that came when the folk from Alabama, Louisiana, Mississippi, Tennessee, Arkansas, Kentucky even came from down south to the north. You know, obviously they were part of that, both I would call the first migration, the first northern migration, which is during Reconstruction, but also the second one after World War II. And then for us, the third one, which would be after, I mean, after World War I, and the third one would be World War Two. There were migrations in that often don't get named that way. But in the Midwest for sure, you can track the migration patterns. I know you were joking a little bit, but but but can you actually tell me what happens when you head back down to the Gulf? Yes, I all of a sudden come alive in the kind of way that I can't ever say the only other place I can kind of speak to this coming alive is when I am on the continent of Africa. There's a kind of way of which everything in me wakens to a kind of reality. And for some of us who are Black and have African ancestry, it's hard to pinpoint a space, a literal space where you have connection to the land and the peoples. Moving to New England, one of the things I noted immediately is there wasn't a familiarity with the black people here that I had. And I still don't. I've been here 23 years now and of course, yeah, okay. I'm from here, no, I live here, right? But I don't have that same sense, but there is an awakening to the space and the place, the way the tone and tenor of the way folk move and talk and be. I don't know those people from anybody, but I know everything about them. I know, I know exactly, I know exactly what you mean. And I don't think, I think everybody from another culture knows what you mean but you have to be in another space where you can observe it in order for you to pick that up. And so are there any other connections that we need to make before we transition to what brought you here 23 years ago? So let me go back one more little nuance to this. There is, I have to say, there's an exception to what I've just said, is when I touched down in Brazil, I had a recognition of what I call, and I'm not saying this to be, let me just say what I said. I turned to my wife and said, lips and hips of Africa. Because Everywhere I looked around, people looked like people I knew. And then of course, doing some of the work that we ended up doing and continue to do. I know now that many, we would be remiss. And in fact, we're misinformed and undereducated, missing educated. The road from Africa came through South America and not directly here. And of course that bears out with the ancestry of so many people that have African ancestry on the continent of South America, but of course, particularly through Brazil. So there is that kind of aliveness also occurs there. And now I know why, because of this familiarity with the peoples. Right. That I can't explain other than I know what I'm talking about And I don't have a reason to try to research it out. I know those folks, their ways. I know their ways, the kind of way that, I mean, just there's so many that can nuance this to death, but just to know that I know their ways. And what did education mean in your family? It meant a lot. I am not first generation college graduate, college attendee or Institute. I have multiple generations of graduates from Southern University, Alcorn State, Jackson State. So yeah, this is, school was very important. Education was very important. And one of my cousins was a he was a deputy superintendent of schools in the Chicago public school system back in the day, etc, etc, etc. So yeah, multiple generations and educated folk and educated preachers, right? So education was very important to my family in many ways. Did your father think that you should get a particular degree? Like, did he want you to get a degree in sociology? Did he want you to get a master's of divinity? Like what kind of education do you think prepared you best for the ministry? So the university of live your life and go preach it out. So, so, so there is a kind of, I don't want to call it this, but you know, educated clergy was not necessarily the, the, the premier thing one would have sought coming along because you had to have the spirit. Yeah. You gotta have the spirit. And that would have been first, do you have the anointing? School was secondary as it related to, you know, my vocation in choral ministry. And yet, because of that, It was also then, you gotta go do that because you've gotta go get your learning. So it was there. My biological father, so I have four sets of, I have four dads. Okay. Because my people, my mama married three dudes, had four marriages with three different dudes. So I have that. And each one of those men had, they played a particular part in my rearing. And then I got adopted into a family where ministry was essential in fact, and lived in the home of my pastor, who was also my adoptive dad. So this, and of course he supported, I went to Bible college and graduated from Bible college. There was all of this education with a center and a centerpiece. So they wanted me to get all the schooling I could get. And so they would tickle pink that one day I came home with a doctorate degree. All right. And then 23 years you've been here. What brought you here? Ministry. Ministry brought me here. I jumped around the country during the 1980s and 90s, early 90s. I helped start it. Well, I went from St. Louis to Colorado, spent some time in Colorado, and we came back to St. Louis, then moved to Florida, Central Florida, for two and a half, three years, and then moved out to the Bay Area in California, spent two, three years there, and then came back home, fell in love in Earthang, And- Fell in love in St. Louis? In St. Louis. Met this young lady who became the apple of my eye, she still is. And we started having kids and ministry still called. I had not quite completed all of my learning and all of my education. The part of my journey particularly is that I spent time as, I am a musician. My grandfather was the preacher, my paternal grandfather was the preacher, my maternal grandmother was the piano player. And so their blood runs in my veins. And so I spent my early first 25 years of life being the musician that preached. And in that capacity, became a songwriter and a music producer. And in 1981, 82, I wrote two songs on a record that won a Grammy and was acknowledged in gospel music. So I have some notice in the gospel music idiom, particularly, and traveled the world, in fact. You know, not only the United States, but was in Europe, touring and doing that kind of stuff. So That's the other part of my journey, which caused me to move to several of these places because I was doing music and ministry together, kind of paired together. And ultimately got a call from a colleague here who said, hey, there's an African-American congregation. And I think, you know, he said, you know, honestly, let me be very selfish in my ask of you. I would love for you to come to Hartford and work with me and let's see what could happen. What's your part of the work? He was a UCC minister in what we call parachurch, in the parachurch context, meaning there are any number of ministries that sit outside of a local church, but also inform the missional work of those local churches in collective ways. And so that colleague was very, very, he was essential in suggesting that I applied to this particular church and made the connections and made the move possible with the, with the grant. Much like we were talking about earlier. I know that tradition of, hey, we want you to be the minister and I'm gonna write a check to help make it happen. And so I know that tradition well. And what made it appealing? So he made the ask, what made it appealing to you? The day he called me, I'm gonna say it was God, because that's how I speak. The day he called me to ask me what I consider, and he kind of timidly, cheapishly, he was very, he was him horn. What you want, man? And so that day I had tenured in written form my resignation from the congregation I was serving in Chicago. And so it was a God thing. It was like, I called my wife. I said, guess what just happened? As I finished penning, literally, my resignation letter and hit send, It went off. Bling, bling, bling, bling, bling, within say 30 minutes, I get a call from this person, you know, saying, hey, would you consider? And I said, okay, what kind of deal? And we started kind of casually talking. He called me back within a few hours and said, here's what I'm thinking about. And that's how early conversations went. That was in March. I was here July 1, 2000. We were rolling into the city of Hartford with my bags in tow and my children and my wife in the car. Ah, how many children by then? Two. Yes, two children. Two children. My daughter was going into the eighth grade and my son was going to be going into kindergarten. So they grew up here in Hartford. While being born in St. Louis, They really grew up here. Had you come to Hartford before for conferences and other... I'd been here before. I've been here to speak, in fact. I'd been here to speak, in fact, preached in faith congregational church the last Sunday of January in 2000. It was, the reason I know this, these facts, it was Super Bowl Sunday. And I dashed, I got on a plane and dashed home to watch in my home, the second half of the Super Bowl with the St. Louis Rams. I was one of the people who was an ardent, outspoken proponent to the then LA Rams moving to St. Louis to become the St. Louis Rams. I thought it was ridiculous that we would be giving a ball team that kind of money as an incentive to move to St. Louis. As I pastored in St. Louis in the mid-90s, only to be rooting them on in 2000 with my Rams jersey on that I slipped into after I left Faith Church. I said, let me put my Ram shirt on as I walked through the airports. Late convert. I was here in Harvard. A late convert, but a convert. Yeah, yeah. You know, fair weathered, let me just tell you. So yes, I had been here I was here probably a year, year and a half before for an event and I was asked to be a speaker at and then I was here one other time. So Hartford was not new to me but I had no idea I'd be coming here. And then my last trip here to speak, I didn't know six months later I'd be here as a resident. All right. So that was 23 years ago. And what were your impressions of the city now that you were a denizen? Well, that's a whole, you don't have enough time or space on this. I need to go into all of that. What I'll say is that as I was characterizing how I, and thank you for probing a bit, when it comes to being in the Gulf region of this country, The peoples, the place, the culture, the environs here in New England couldn't be further from what I knew or understood. And so it took me some time, both in a kind of deeply reflective and spiritual context to say, God, what am I doing here and why? You know, trying to answer those questions, both for myself, But also for the context of the work that I felt like I was called to do. The folk here are very different from the kinds of people I've known, that I grew up with. I have a, you know, 23 years later, you know, I've come to regard and of course love, you know, the folk and the people I serve here. And yet it is still very different. And I'm able to take it, take steps back and to try to get a sense of what that is and what characterizes that, that difference. Yeah. Let's hear, let's hear a little bit of that. Let's hear a little bit of that. So, so, would Back up one second. So would you say when we're describing it, are we calling it, is it a New England sensibility or is it a Connecticut sensibility? What is it? Yeah, it's a New England sensibility, but Connecticut has its own dynamics. I have also encountered folk, and I'm going to say black folk in particular, in Massachusetts. You know, ain't too many black folk in Vermont, New Hampshire, and Maine today. Ain't too many of them. And Rhode Island has a dozen or more, but that's about it. So the largest, you know, communities of black, and I would say black and brown folks, are around the urban centers in both Connecticut and Massachusetts, but it has been in my experience within the context of all of New England, this idea. And I'll say a couple of things about that I have observed. One, the system of enslavement derived differently and manifested differently here in New England than it did in the South. In the South, you had large, what I would call prison enslavement work camps, aka, some people call them plantations. I can't use a nice word for what, you know, was the experience of ancestors of mine. These were, you know, work camps, right? And that was different here in the North and in particular in New England. There weren't large bodies of these groups of folk who had to live and work and be social with each other in a way. So they had to create communities. Here, the enslavement period was characterized by maybe a mom, dad, a one or two, and they had children. But they all lived in the house of one family. So this communal, the way the communities developed was very different. Which speaks to what I encountered 250, 300 years later. Folks who, you know, Folks who don't have a sense of community in the same way that's experienced in the south. So people are just not friendly. My beloved Pamela, she named it early on, she was able to characterize it and put language around it for me. She said, this place is, the folk here, they major on alienation, isolation and aloneness. There's a, you know, and the Yankee-ness of it all, I can do it on my own, this kind of strident, rugged individualism that is a kind of one major Yankee, New England, Connecticut thing. It seems to make sense. So so that is health, at least for me, as I characterize being here. And Yeah, I mean, I've done some further research and conversation to others around that. And they seem to do just like you. You know, even if they don't know the history that I'm pulling into that, it seems to hold true. Right, Yeah. That is one of the primary things that comes from West Indians when they first come here. This is what I hear in the oral histories, that that sense of duty, obligation, and responsibility for each other is very different. And It's not just about having a critical mass of massive people. It's just really interesting how it can take on this regional, this regional aspect to it and sort of live on in in the way that black people experience each other in other places. That's right. So how do you promote community and connection and communalism when you're like fighting against the cultural tide in New England? Well, yeah, how, I don't know. You know, I show up and be who I be. I'll say it that way. So I think a couple of things. The uniqueness of who I am as a clergy, as a pastor, as a faith leader, and I would say a spiritual practitioner, calls for wherever I am to be concerned with how do we be with each other? How do we do the thing that we need to do together? Whatever that is, whether that's a project or as pastor of a community of faith, but also within the broader context of a community to talk about, what does it mean to be with each other? Here we are, we have similar, we have some mutual interest, don't we? Well, then how do we enliven the resources that we all bring to the table in order to make a better place for not just me and mine, but you and yours, and how about Pookie and Ray Ray and everybody on the block in this side of town? How about the whole town here in Hartford? And how might Hartford connect to New Haven and Waterbury and New London and Bridgeport, where they are similarly set in both terms of population and resources and the lack of power, political, social, economic, et cetera. So I've been engaged in these conversations, you know? Not just engaged in conversations, starting organizations and groups and convening stuff. And I have a 23 year track record engaged in this very thing. The question you're asking is a serious one. I take serious anyway, and have engaged in it from the very beginning. And then who was your primer on Hartford now that you were going to live here? Who gave you 101? Yeah, well, so the colleague that actually worked very hard for me to be here, his name is the Reverend Donald R. Steinle. May he rest in peace. Don was the executive director for the Christian Activities Council. It has now become the Center for Leadership and Justice. But he was the person that said, hey, you need to be here and I'm gonna try to make it happen. And it was his leadership. We had met each other a number of years earlier as I came to the United Church of Christ, probably a decade before, a little less than that, but, and got into a big argument about, you know, I was a little put off that this white dude was talking about what black folk ought to do in the city. I thought he needed to shut up. And I said that too, I was young and brash. And that was probably our first meeting of each other. And he then engaged me in conversation. And from that time till when he made transition, we became the best of friends and trusted colleagues and friends and brothers. Because I didn't know at the time, his commitment to the city, came to know it. And he was the person that said, hey, okay, this is how I see Hartford. How did you see it? What was his view? How did he see Hartford? He saw many of the, he saw what I think are, you know, at one level, what's here. It is a city that at one point was the jewel city of the Industrial Revolution. 150, 60 years ago, this was the spot. The Travelers Insurance Building was one of the highest buildings in the world. This was the publishing book capital, the publishing capital of the United States. It probably was, a hundred years ago, it probably was one of the wealthiest places. With all this paper money flowing in and through here, we call it, generated by what we now call the insurance industry. All this speculation produced a great deal of wealth that then seeded that industrial revolution. So that, you know, this place is where Samuel Clemens and Harry Beecher Stowe, you know, made their, you know, benefited greatly from being here. That then you look at, you know, coming into the, you know, the 21st century, this was one of the poorest cities in the nation. Yeah. And remains that way. And the questions that he asked, I began to ask in similar ways, what happened? And of course, everybody comes out of that hopefully, with their own versions of what happened. And you put that together and then you try to work on, for me, after you get through talking to all this highfalutin intellectual stuff, and I love doing it, they're real people here and I'm a pastor. So at the end of the day, I want to know how, you know, Lucuicia and Carmenita, how they gonna live? You know, and have been able to serve in a number of capacities, both in ministry and in social work, which I spent a good deal of my time before coming into the academy as a college administrator, doing the same stuff. I'm doing the same kinds of things, trying to pay attention to the people and serve the folks who are what I would call the least of these. What do you call them? The least of these. That's a biblical term that Jesus would have spoken of in the 25th chapter of Matthew in particular. And what answer did you come to as to why? For Hartford, which part of the answer that you came to now informs your ministry? For sure. It's service to the community that was left behind as progression and wealth, you know, dominated the field and left in its wake, you know, what, okay, so now I'm talking my language, right? And I'm using, I'm talking, you know, from a particular lens. I believe capitalism leaves in its wake the haves and the have-nots. In fact, it has to have the have-nots in order for the haves to have. And so where the have-nots exist, the negotiated peace that exists with the haves and the have-nots and here That took the form of you know in the early early in mid 20th century that took the form of the bishops, who were the corporate executives, largely of the insurance companies, that saw after Hartford, that the political structure wasn't that strong, but they placated to the corporate interest. And it was that corporate interest that saw after the welfare of, you know, Hartford, Bridgeport, Waterbury and New Haven. It also didn't hurt that in New Haven's direction, it had that school just that's right in the middle of that town and the kind of exposure, both of wealth and academic rigor that Yale creates in the wake of a town. But of course, that has some up for some, but in my estimation, it also has a whole lot of down for the folk who live in those spaces. And O'Heartford has his version of that too. Right, yeah, Right. So what the bishops were doing is now called some version of corporate social responsibility. Correct. Very paternalistic to think that it is the responsibility of the CEOs and executives of the corporate world that need to take care of the overall arching needs of people in the city. So I wondered what your views were of that. Well, so I would characterize it, of course, absolutely. The engineering that goes into, That's why I call it the negotiated peace, right? There's been a way in which that's got, that had the mediation of what I call gatekeepers who were able to speak for and cut the deals with the bishops. So there was a relationship that while paternalistic, of course, had some, there was somebody in the ear of those folk, right? And so, you know, to be a gatekeeper, you know, representing your folk and your kind was a legitimate form of leadership in our communities and in these desperate communities. That form of leadership and that form of the navigated negotiations of that and the exchange of that changes. So that form of leadership that still is practiced here doesn't work anymore for millennials, for example. They don't even know who those gatekeepers were and why should they listen to them anyway? Because one of the dynamics of that gatekeeping form, for example, in my opinion, was that they took care of their kids first. You know, it was the folk that was closest to them. They weren't necessarily the ones both either deserving or the most competent, for example. So you see my thinking. I got lots to say. I would have lots more to say about, you know, that was underwritten by redlining, you know, 100 years ago into the, probably the 1950s. There were also some downtowns here in Connecticut. And so you got all of that social engineering that went along. Then you got just like in every other place here in the United States, the interstates came in. So there was also the kind of socioeconomic, what I would call gerrymandering, that went on with projects like, federal projects like the interstate system and other forms of that, that further isolated these communities and the peoples. So. So the, it's sort of interesting because the, There, this sense of community that you talk about, that you didn't necessarily see. So then thinking about it from the perspective of the Native Black community here, right? Like they also speak in those terms that they had, you know, that they had community and that they are protecting all of the gains, very small gains, but all of the gains that they had been able to make, and that the 20th century was just a completely different ballgame, right? Because you have significant African-American ownership of real estate in the 19th century, into the 20th century, And then things start going in the wrong direction. And then all of that is eaten away in the 1920s and in the 1930s because of the economic downturn, just in time for new generations and new waves of migrants, both from the Caribbean and from the South, to come in and sort of throw everything up in the air when, as you say, red lining is well, well underway when the black community has been essentially kicked out of the South end, right? And so, yeah, so I wonder what you make of that, the fact that the native New Englanders, native folks in Connecticut also tell the story of like a community that was lost. So, Yes, and I would say I agree. I think it is the dynamics of that history and those histories, they are buried, but they have some common themes and similar circumstances. And one of the questions I asked as a 21st century resident is what happened to that 19th century, you know, fervor for freedom and liberty and for ownership, and being able to dictate my own terms, right? What happened to that liberative fervor that existed in the 19th century and into the early 20th century, that then there seemed to be a complete erasure of it by the 21st century when I arrived here. So that there's no element or very little remnant of that, that exists, that I had to dig in, I had to get into these stacks of books to find where that is, right? You got to find, I found a lot of hand-me-down buildings. Now I'm talking, now I'm getting ready to get messy. I'm talking about churches. Very few of these churches were the churches that the black folk built, while they were towered a history that dates 200 years, faith congregational church through Talcott Street, through the first colored congregational church, because that was the first name of that church. That began at Center Church, our first church congregation, first church congregation downtown, right? That they towered there, we were the first, but where's the building? Where were the program? Where is the memory? Where's the marker? You know? Yeah, where's the historical marker? Where's the institutions that were built? You know, what we were remarking on with that, the Rogers Project, Lord have mercy. That seems like that's been a forever project that I'll believe it when I see it. That's what I said. I said the scaffolding is gonna go up and it's gone up before. Yes, so, and there seems to be the ebb and flow of that. So you'll get some progenitors, you'll get some people who are trying to lead and galvanize that effort, but then it's like elastic. That thing will expand out to subsume us, right? To accommodate us for the period of time we punching and pushing and pulling and grabbing and doing. And then when we go, when we either move on in all kinds of ways, it goes back to a place and almost erasure of both what existed prior to, but also what existed during that period of time. And then we're towering still the first. Yeah, I was the first, blah, blah, blah. I was the first, we were the first, blah, blah, blah. And for me, I come from folk who were also first, but three generations ago. Right. Not last week, you the first, you know, in my lifetime, the first black mayor served here. Consecutive black male mayor, black female mayor, and then nothing else through the 1980s. And they're voting tonight. They are voting tonight of who do support. Yep, that it'll be interesting to see who the town committee thinks deserves the mantle, but I don't think it's going to be any black person. It's not. I guess I shouldn't say that on camera. No, it's not. We'll find, because then we can just attach the news headline. It's not like we don't... Who's the black candidate in the mayoral election? It's just a fact. It is, it is. And for me, Doc, I lament far more than I could tell you these self-evident truths. Here we are. I said it to when the current mayor, his wife, it's interesting, I've been speaking in a number of settings, she was in the room or in the space And she didn't like me very much. And probably the feeling is mutual. Because I say, how in the Sam Hades, in 20 anything, in a town that is a chocolate town, that we have a white boy mayor, but not just this town, not just Hartford, all of our major urban centers, that they are all chocolate towns. They have majority black and brown populations. And yet we're all currently right now all governed by white male mayors. There's something happening in the municipal electoral political system. Right? But that's just a symptom of a wider, larger, broader, I would call problem. So what kind of leadership structure do you think is needed now? Because we talked about the era of paternalism and the gatekeeping and the people who at least had the air and those people are the power brokers. And if anything is happening in the Black community, you know the one person to call or the three people to call and But here we are now requiring very different model of leadership and a vision and I don't Currently see anybody With it Doc if I knew to answer that question, I would show Beaton wrote that book. I'd be hocking that book. I'd be on everybody's talk show, everywhere, talking about it. I don't know. I'm the first to tell you, I don't know. I'm not, that's not also an admission that I'm willing to give up on the idea. I think we do need leadership. I think we do need multiple conversations at a number, on a number of different levels and in a number of different spaces and places throughout our communities, how do we... How do... What... Okay. So, the first thing I think we got to deal with is who the we is. We don't know who the we are. Because you know, one example of this is the seeming disconnection between the Caribbean community and the quote African American community. That I'm even saying that without a recognition that we all are African and have our at least African roots and ancestry. And it's common, some commonness with that. But that's just one idea of it. It's everywhere that I see. Again, here we are. Here's this alienation, isolation and aloneness. You know, this strident, independent, you know, individualism without an understanding that we are codependent on each other. The same police that pull me over are going to pull you over. And they're not going to ask the question, you from Jamaica? Or you from Trinidad? Or you from South Carolina? Right? They're not asking those questions. I asked this question. We had a very interesting conversation with some, they would refer to themselves as Southeast Asians. And when we started to talk about, you know, what are our commonalities? They were like, well, I'm not, you know, okay. But you dark, I want, you know, your skin dark as me. In fact, in some cases, you're darker skin than I am. Right? And you get pulled over just like I get pulled over in certain neighborhoods, don't you? Well, they had to agree to that. And do they ask you, are you Southeast Asian? No, they don't ask me. And do you feel like you get mistreated or treated well? Well, then, you know, it gets, they have similar, they have similar, you know, experiences, But don't talk about it in a public sense. So I say all that to say, we got a lot of work to do about who the we is before we can even, before we get grounds of commonality. And it has been a tireless and thankless, in many instances, bit of the work to even begin to have these kinds of conversations in community. I'll tell you one other thing. I was invited by some colleagues to a couple of election cycles ago to have some conversations about voting and the ballot, right? And so the project determined that we would have these conversations in barbershops and beauty salons. And We set up a number of them across the state. I did two of them, but was in conversation with my colleagues who were doing a number of these. And one of the interesting things is I asked, this was overtly political conversation, right? The question was, who's registered to vote? Not everybody had an opinion. Let me be clear, everybody, as they would say back where I'm from, everybody. Everybody had an opinion. Everybody had a thought, an idea. Then when I asked the question, who's here registered to vote? Two thirds of those people could not raise their hand. Of that one third that's left, who voted in the last, okay, last three, four elections, either federal, statewide, or municipal? Those were down, down, and down for people who actually participated actively. But they had opinions. And so I said to them, then you are the tail of the tape in many instances. You have opinions, you have ideas, you think it ought to go. And yet the way in this particular brand of governance, you don't participate. Why? Boy, them answers, it get quiet and crickets, right? And everybody got an individual story around it. And let me also say it's not made easy within the context of our community and the laws related to that and the practices, right? But notwithstanding that, that's a problem. That's a core, that's a key problem. And so, And I also get that it serves somebody to make sure you keep them voting rolls down, right? And you can anticipate who's gonna vote for you. And you make sure you get them leaders in your pocket. And so incumbency then is a thing. And incumbents are not beaten in the political process very often. And I'd say that holds true both in a political context, but also in a corporate context. These people in the church, they're pastorates that are 40, 30, 25, 30, 40 years in the same pulpit. So those are little victims that are created. Now I'm talking about my own, I'm talking about my own, you know, and what that actually means for how power is both amassed, accumulated, and then distributed and shared. So those are some of my ideas. Uh-oh, I'm dead. Oh, no, I had muted you. Okay. Yeah, no, that's very interesting. And So there are people who are served by every single social ill that we face in this country. Poverty makes other people wealthy, right? Not us. Not voting, you know, serves the incumbents. I mean, we, a lot of people sat out in the last election cycle. A lot of people sat out. When they could have voted for Hillary, a lot of people sat out. And so I'm up, but that makes me optimistic though. It makes me optimistic because that gap, you can bridge that gap, right? You can get people registered to vote. You can get people to turn out. Because when you paint the system as ogres and monsters and it just seems like there's nothing you can do, It's like, well, in fact, they're not ogres, monsters, or aliens. These are very much human created systems. And if they're human created systems, then we as humans can engage them, right? Because sometimes the problems are so overwhelming, you might as well not do anything because there's nothing you can't do. But voting really does make a difference. Registering people to vote really makes a difference. Showing up really does make a difference. And When I'm beating the drum, that's what I say. Like my students and I know you must be getting this with your students. And my students, they come, they're idealistic. And then college seems to beat it out of them. Yep. Yep. They said they can't. They think they can change the world and they leave college going Oh, the problems are too much. But now I say to my students not aliens This is not Independence Day. It's not aliens. It's not ogres. It's not some strange beast that we are fighting. It's things that we know. It's greed. It's capitalism. It's incumbency. It's not having term limits for senators. It's people who feel like they own their office. That's right. So hopefully, you know, when my students are asking me about leading, like what sort of leadership we need, I said, everybody doesn't need to be a leader. Right, right, right. Everybody does not need to be a leader. That's right. That's right. I say to my folks, I use religious terms, what we have now and the systems that we have now did not come out of heaven. We created them. Yes. And I'm using the word we, all of us participated in a process that created these systems that we now have. And we are now gifted with the opportunity. If in fact it is wrong and we feel like we are being wronged by the systems, since those systems were created by human beings, just like you and me, we can then deconstruct these systems and then reconstruct them in a way that makes sense for us here in the 21st century. Can't we do that? That's my cry, as I have, that I begin to say, and I didn't finish, I realized I didn't finish the talk, when I'd make the criticism that how could this, how could a white dude be the mayor of a chocolate town. And you not ask him on a daily basis, what are you doing to fix what's wrong in my neighborhood? To fix what's wrong. How do we have decades of ineffective education, public education, while we have allegedly some of the most highly sought-after spaces of education in our, these schools of higher education. Yale, Trinity, UConn. What? You mean tell me we can't produce, you know, folk who could teach in these systems and could share. I mean, just education alone, healthcare, the same thing. Oh, no, social work. I mean, if you go anywhere, it's a bunch of white women leading the effort around serving the brothers and sisters of color in these systems who vastly outnumber anybody else. I can keep going on this line. You know this is what you're dealing with. And I say, and then you seem to be okay with it. Well, I'm not okay. I'm dissatisfied with this status quo. And I intend to and will continue to use my voice, my effort, my will, my energy toward unpacking and dismantling that job. It just, I cannot abide it. And I'm not going to be quiet about it. I haven't. And what I like about this generation is we got, it's YouTubable. You can Google my name and you can find stuff out about where I've been, you know, spending my time and using my capital, whatever it is, my social capital in that regard. It is, it's up to us. And, you know, as we struggle with who the ussy is, we've got to do, you know, it's gotta be better. And each of us have to make the commitment to do better ourselves. Right. You know, both individually and then in small groups. My family votes and I stayed on my children as they became into adulthood, they all registered. My granddaughter is getting ready to turn 18. Guess where she's going to? One of the first places she's going to go is to go get her voting, you're gonna get a voter registration card and sign up to vote. You can't live in my house and not be a registered voter. And then when voting day occurs, we talk about what's on the ballot, And we talk about what are the pros and cons. I typically am involved in some of those conversations, at least over the last 20 plus years here locally. And so we're gonna talk about it a bit. And that's family conversation at the dinner table and at the lunch table. And when we're in the living room talking about nothing, we start talking about something. And this is part of that something we start talking about. All right, so I just have one, sort of like one question to kind of wrap things up, which is that sort of in the tradition of the city missionary movement, in the tradition of Christian Activities Council, right, and religious leaders playing a really important role in all of Hartford's history. I wonder what you think about that kind of gatekeeping and spiritual, it's not necessarily spiritual, right? But moral authority. What role does that moral authority play in this like ferment we've been talking about, especially since they took Christian Activities Council and turned it into leadership and justice? Yeah, I think a lot about it. I have some, yeah, I think a lot about it. In a place where the YMCA, YWCA movement began here in Hartford, where the Children's Hospital started, where Hartford Hospital got its start amongst religious folk. The very beginnings of most of the systems of the social network of care, all began in faith communities, vis-a-vis the churches and synagogues here in Hartford and in Connecticut. And I would say that in larger ways across the country, right? The first schools, Yale was the school to train ministers. Trinity College got started to do the very same thing. So I think it has a, and when you start referencing that, I mean, our child welfare systems got started because churches took on the effort through the city missional society, which became Christian Activities Council did that. So I have a lot, I think it has a lot to say about how it is to lead. And one of my disappointments and one of my criticisms has been it is not taking seriously that history and tried to understand how do we create change and how do we lead, not just be a player, how do we lead? And that leadership means some certain things. And I think in many instances, that leadership has taken the form of charismatic leaders. Not just one, but many, a number of them who were working in concert with and supportive of each other to help build whatever the will is to get things done. And I'm disappointed that here we are with all of what our communities are facing. And we're taking baby steps. That there isn't, for example, always doing an election season, for example. That we are not leading the effort of what is being talked about. We're not even in the conversation. We haven't called nobody together to put them on notice. Et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. And I can be, well, I can't be, because I've been one of the people that have been in the rooms to say, nope, while the strikers were striking, the state workers were striking and taking a rest at the Capitol a month ago or so more, I was inside talking to the governor and the speaker. So I was one of those faith leaders. I take seriously the bully pulpit, if you will, that I have, The role that I serve in, I do take that serious. And I mean, you know, 2020, while the whole country was turnt up around police, you know, reform, police accountability, We leaned in our faith and called it fast and was on the state capital grounds for nearly two weeks saying, y'all need to come in here and let's deal with this. You know, why did the Black Lives Matter all over the state, as it is all over the country. Y'all better make it matter here. And let's get some policy work done. Not just going to a protest and feeling good for a moment. Let's get some policy done. Let's get some laws passed. And we leaned in our faith to help to create a culture for that kind of policy work to get done. So I'm disappointed that others of my colleagues don't take that, not that they gotta do what I do, but do something, do something and lead instead of following. So that's, I probably said too much then, but you know. No, and just, and let's close out with the Moral Mondays overview. Sure, so Moral Monday CT really got started out of our personal commitment to see what was going to happen and be a part of the change in Ferguson. Black Lives Matter took on, you know, legs and feet and became a thing coming out of Ferguson. And so we were back and forth to St. Louis, my wife and I in particular, you know, to be lend our support there at home. And came back here, you know, was reporting, got invited to a couple of meetings to report out, hey, you just back from the front, the movement front and said, listen, I'm no more willing to just sit idly by while we do the same thing, go meet with city fathers and mothers and do the same old, same old. And so we hit the streets and in 2014, 2015, led the first Black Lives Matter rally publicly. And 17 of us got arrested for Black Lives Matter here in the state of Connecticut and at downtown Hartford. And that began, that was the big catalyst that put Moral Monday CT on the map, where we bridged and we really created the nexus for us, all of the social justice issues that are of moral import, that the Moral Monday movement was taking on, you know, from, you know, education and mass incarceration and employment and you name it, that broad swath of what I would call social justice stuff. We married it to the movement for black lives and black lives matter. And so it has taken on, what we've done is done that being informed by faith. Those of us who are followers of Jesus, Christians, and Muslims, you know, Jews, those have no faith, but have a sense of what's right and wrong. We would call it, I would call that a kind of moral sensibility. We've tried to tap into that and say, good folk with wishing people well, can come and have some conversations about what it means to do better than we are doing now. And that conversation continues. We are, you know, which has led us to be involved in a number of things. Electorally, I think there is an argument to be made for rank choice voting, for example. And we've lended our voice and support there. The continued conversation about what happens to us here in Connecticut because we're we're not in danger of rolling back real way. But what does it mean as it relates to states like New York and others who are having challenges in that regard? So I'll name those two, but there are a couple of others that certainly get our attention as we continue to lead from the space as co-founder and I guess director of More Money CT, co-director of More Money CT. All right. Well, thank you for that overview. I really really appreciate you taking the time You're welcome. This is you make it easy doc so I appreciate it All right

lines.line: # Introduction and Permissions

> The interviewer, Dr. Fiona, introduces the oral history session with Bishop John Selders, noting the date and location. Bishop Selders provides his full name, titles, and grants permission for the recording. This sets the stage for a formal and open conversation about his life and experiences.

metadata.title: Introduction and Permissions
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer, Dr. Fiona, introduces the oral history session with Bishop John Selders, noting the date and location. Bishop Selders provides his full name, titles, and grants permission for the recording. This sets the stage for a formal and open conversation about his life and experiences.

lines.line: # Family Background and Early Childhood

> Bishop Selders describes his deep roots in the Pentecostal tradition, highlighting his family's multigenerational involvement in church leadership. He shares details about his grandparents, their roles in the Church of God in Christ, and his upbringing in Kinloch, Missouri, the first chartered African American town in the state. The narrative includes family structure, early influences, and the significance of church as the 'family business.'

metadata.title: Family Background and Early Childhood
metadata.synopsis: Bishop Selders describes his deep roots in the Pentecostal tradition, highlighting his family's multigenerational involvement in church leadership. He shares details about his grandparents, their roles in the Church of God in Christ, and his upbringing in Kinloch, Missouri, the first chartered African American town in the state. The narrative includes family structure, early influences, and the significance of church as the 'family business.'

lines.line: # Migration, Ancestral Roots, and Cultural Identity

> The speaker delves into his family's migration from the South, tracing both paternal and maternal roots to Louisiana and Mississippi. He reflects on the cultural and spiritual resonance he feels when visiting the Gulf region and draws parallels to his experiences in Brazil and Africa. These reflections highlight the enduring connections to ancestral lands, the impact of migration patterns, and the ways in which Southern culture and identity persist in northern cities.

metadata.title: Migration, Ancestral Roots, and Cultural Identity
metadata.synopsis: The speaker delves into his family's migration from the South, tracing both paternal and maternal roots to Louisiana and Mississippi. He reflects on the cultural and spiritual resonance he feels when visiting the Gulf region and draws parallels to his experiences in Brazil and Africa. These reflections highlight the enduring connections to ancestral lands, the impact of migration patterns, and the ways in which Southern culture and identity persist in northern cities.

lines.line: # Education and Its Role in the Family

> Education is portrayed as a central value in Bishop Selders' family, with multiple generations attending and graduating from historically Black colleges and universities. He discusses the expectations placed on him regarding education, the balance between spiritual calling and formal learning, and the influence of various father figures and mentors in shaping his educational and ministerial path. The importance of both academic and spiritual preparation for leadership is emphasized.

metadata.title: Education and Its Role in the Family
metadata.synopsis: Education is portrayed as a central value in Bishop Selders' family, with multiple generations attending and graduating from historically Black colleges and universities. He discusses the expectations placed on him regarding education, the balance between spiritual calling and formal learning, and the influence of various father figures and mentors in shaping his educational and ministerial path. The importance of both academic and spiritual preparation for leadership is emphasized.

lines.line: # Journey to Hartford and Early Impressions

> Bishop Selders recounts the series of moves that led him to Hartford, including his work as a musician, songwriter, and minister in various states. He describes the circumstances of his call to Hartford, the role of a colleague in facilitating his move, and his family's transition to the city. He also shares his initial experiences visiting Hartford and his early impressions upon becoming a resident, including the cultural adjustments required.

metadata.title: Journey to Hartford and Early Impressions
metadata.synopsis: Bishop Selders recounts the series of moves that led him to Hartford, including his work as a musician, songwriter, and minister in various states. He describes the circumstances of his call to Hartford, the role of a colleague in facilitating his move, and his family's transition to the city. He also shares his initial experiences visiting Hartford and his early impressions upon becoming a resident, including the cultural adjustments required.

lines.line: # Cultural Differences and Community in New England

> The speaker reflects on the stark cultural differences between New England and the regions where he grew up, particularly regarding community, friendliness, and social cohesion. He discusses the historical roots of these differences, including the nature of enslavement in New England versus the South, and the resulting impact on Black communal life. The challenges of adapting to and understanding the New England sensibility are explored, as well as the ongoing process of building relationships in a new context.

metadata.title: Cultural Differences and Community in New England
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the stark cultural differences between New England and the regions where he grew up, particularly regarding community, friendliness, and social cohesion. He discusses the historical roots of these differences, including the nature of enslavement in New England versus the South, and the resulting impact on Black communal life. The challenges of adapting to and understanding the New England sensibility are explored, as well as the ongoing process of building relationships in a new context.

lines.line: # Building Community and Leadership in Hartford

> Bishop Selders discusses his approach to fostering community and connection in Hartford, emphasizing the importance of showing up authentically and encouraging collective action. He describes the role of key individuals, such as Reverend Donald R. Stanley, in helping him acclimate to the city and understand its dynamics. The conversation highlights the need for intentional efforts to build communal ties and the ongoing work required to create a sense of belonging and shared purpose.

metadata.title: Building Community and Leadership in Hartford
metadata.synopsis: Bishop Selders discusses his approach to fostering community and connection in Hartford, emphasizing the importance of showing up authentically and encouraging collective action. He describes the role of key individuals, such as Reverend Donald R. Stanley, in helping him acclimate to the city and understand its dynamics. The conversation highlights the need for intentional efforts to build communal ties and the ongoing work required to create a sense of belonging and shared purpose.

lines.line: # Hartford’s Socioeconomic History and Power Structures

> The discussion turns to Hartford's transformation from a wealthy industrial hub to one of the nation's poorest cities. Bishop Selders analyzes the roles of corporate executives, political structures, and the legacy of capitalism in shaping the city's fortunes. He critiques the paternalistic model of corporate social responsibility, the influence of gatekeepers, and the impact of redlining, sundown towns, and federal projects like the interstate system on the city's Black and working-class communities.

metadata.title: Hartford’s Socioeconomic History and Power Structures
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to Hartford's transformation from a wealthy industrial hub to one of the nation's poorest cities. Bishop Selders analyzes the roles of corporate executives, political structures, and the legacy of capitalism in shaping the city's fortunes. He critiques the paternalistic model of corporate social responsibility, the influence of gatekeepers, and the impact of redlining, sundown towns, and federal projects like the interstate system on the city's Black and working-class communities.

lines.line: # The Black Community’s Historical Trajectory in Hartford

> The conversation explores the historical trajectory of Hartford's Black community, including the loss of property, institutions, and a sense of collective agency. Bishop Selders questions the disappearance of 19th-century fervor for freedom and self-determination, noting the challenges of preserving and building upon past achievements. He observes the lack of visible markers and institutions from earlier eras and reflects on the cyclical nature of progress and erasure within the community.

metadata.title: The Black Community’s Historical Trajectory in Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The conversation explores the historical trajectory of Hartford's Black community, including the loss of property, institutions, and a sense of collective agency. Bishop Selders questions the disappearance of 19th-century fervor for freedom and self-determination, noting the challenges of preserving and building upon past achievements. He observes the lack of visible markers and institutions from earlier eras and reflects on the cyclical nature of progress and erasure within the community.

lines.line: # Contemporary Leadership, Political Engagement, and Challenges

> Bishop Selders addresses the complexities of leadership in the current era, highlighting the fragmentation within Black and Caribbean communities and the difficulties in building consensus. He discusses low voter participation, the persistence of incumbency, and the ways in which social and political systems serve entrenched interests. The conversation touches on the importance of civic engagement, the need for new models of leadership, and the challenges of mobilizing collective action in the face of systemic barriers.

metadata.title: Contemporary Leadership, Political Engagement, and Challenges
metadata.synopsis: Bishop Selders addresses the complexities of leadership in the current era, highlighting the fragmentation within Black and Caribbean communities and the difficulties in building consensus. He discusses low voter participation, the persistence of incumbency, and the ways in which social and political systems serve entrenched interests. The conversation touches on the importance of civic engagement, the need for new models of leadership, and the challenges of mobilizing collective action in the face of systemic barriers.

lines.line: # The Role of Faith and Moral Authority in Social Change

> The speaker reflects on the historical significance of faith communities and religious organizations in founding social institutions and leading social change in Hartford. He expresses disappointment that contemporary faith leaders are not as proactive or influential in addressing current challenges. Bishop Selders emphasizes the potential for moral authority to drive policy change and social justice, drawing on his own experiences of leveraging his position to advocate for community needs and systemic reform.

metadata.title: The Role of Faith and Moral Authority in Social Change
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the historical significance of faith communities and religious organizations in founding social institutions and leading social change in Hartford. He expresses disappointment that contemporary faith leaders are not as proactive or influential in addressing current challenges. Bishop Selders emphasizes the potential for moral authority to drive policy change and social justice, drawing on his own experiences of leveraging his position to advocate for community needs and systemic reform.

lines.line: # Moral Mondays, Black Lives Matter, and Activism

> Bishop Selders recounts the origins and evolution of the Moral Mondays movement in Connecticut, rooted in his and his wife's involvement in the Ferguson protests and the rise of Black Lives Matter. He describes the integration of faith-based activism with broader social justice issues, the coalition-building across religious and secular lines, and the movement's impact on policy and electoral reform. The chapter concludes with reflections on ongoing efforts to create meaningful change and the importance of sustained activism.

metadata.title: Moral Mondays, Black Lives Matter, and Activism
metadata.synopsis: Bishop Selders recounts the origins and evolution of the Moral Mondays movement in Connecticut, rooted in his and his wife's involvement in the Ferguson protests and the rise of Black Lives Matter. He describes the integration of faith-based activism with broader social justice issues, the coalition-building across religious and secular lines, and the movement's impact on policy and electoral reform. The chapter concludes with reflections on ongoing efforts to create meaningful change and the importance of sustained activism.

children.text: # Introduction and Permissions

> The interviewer, Dr. Fiona, introduces the oral history session with Bishop John Selders, noting the date and location. Bishop Selders provides his full name, titles, and grants permission for the recording. This sets the stage for a formal and open conversation about his life and experiences.
# Family Background and Early Childhood

> Bishop Selders describes his deep roots in the Pentecostal tradition, highlighting his family's multigenerational involvement in church leadership. He shares details about his grandparents, their roles in the Church of God in Christ, and his upbringing in Kinloch, Missouri, the first chartered African American town in the state. The narrative includes family structure, early influences, and the significance of church as the 'family business.'
# Migration, Ancestral Roots, and Cultural Identity

> The speaker delves into his family's migration from the South, tracing both paternal and maternal roots to Louisiana and Mississippi. He reflects on the cultural and spiritual resonance he feels when visiting the Gulf region and draws parallels to his experiences in Brazil and Africa. These reflections highlight the enduring connections to ancestral lands, the impact of migration patterns, and the ways in which Southern culture and identity persist in northern cities.
# Education and Its Role in the Family

> Education is portrayed as a central value in Bishop Selders' family, with multiple generations attending and graduating from historically Black colleges and universities. He discusses the expectations placed on him regarding education, the balance between spiritual calling and formal learning, and the influence of various father figures and mentors in shaping his educational and ministerial path. The importance of both academic and spiritual preparation for leadership is emphasized.
# Journey to Hartford and Early Impressions

> Bishop Selders recounts the series of moves that led him to Hartford, including his work as a musician, songwriter, and minister in various states. He describes the circumstances of his call to Hartford, the role of a colleague in facilitating his move, and his family's transition to the city. He also shares his initial experiences visiting Hartford and his early impressions upon becoming a resident, including the cultural adjustments required.
# Cultural Differences and Community in New England

> The speaker reflects on the stark cultural differences between New England and the regions where he grew up, particularly regarding community, friendliness, and social cohesion. He discusses the historical roots of these differences, including the nature of enslavement in New England versus the South, and the resulting impact on Black communal life. The challenges of adapting to and understanding the New England sensibility are explored, as well as the ongoing process of building relationships in a new context.
# Building Community and Leadership in Hartford

> Bishop Selders discusses his approach to fostering community and connection in Hartford, emphasizing the importance of showing up authentically and encouraging collective action. He describes the role of key individuals, such as Reverend Donald R. Stanley, in helping him acclimate to the city and understand its dynamics. The conversation highlights the need for intentional efforts to build communal ties and the ongoing work required to create a sense of belonging and shared purpose.
# Hartford’s Socioeconomic History and Power Structures

> The discussion turns to Hartford's transformation from a wealthy industrial hub to one of the nation's poorest cities. Bishop Selders analyzes the roles of corporate executives, political structures, and the legacy of capitalism in shaping the city's fortunes. He critiques the paternalistic model of corporate social responsibility, the influence of gatekeepers, and the impact of redlining, sundown towns, and federal projects like the interstate system on the city's Black and working-class communities.
# The Black Community’s Historical Trajectory in Hartford

> The conversation explores the historical trajectory of Hartford's Black community, including the loss of property, institutions, and a sense of collective agency. Bishop Selders questions the disappearance of 19th-century fervor for freedom and self-determination, noting the challenges of preserving and building upon past achievements. He observes the lack of visible markers and institutions from earlier eras and reflects on the cyclical nature of progress and erasure within the community.
# Contemporary Leadership, Political Engagement, and Challenges

> Bishop Selders addresses the complexities of leadership in the current era, highlighting the fragmentation within Black and Caribbean communities and the difficulties in building consensus. He discusses low voter participation, the persistence of incumbency, and the ways in which social and political systems serve entrenched interests. The conversation touches on the importance of civic engagement, the need for new models of leadership, and the challenges of mobilizing collective action in the face of systemic barriers.

> The speaker reflects on the historical significance of faith communities and religious organizations in founding social institutions and leading social change in Hartford. He expresses disappointment that contemporary faith leaders are not as proactive or influential in addressing current challenges. Bishop Selders emphasizes the potential for moral authority to drive policy change and social justice, drawing on his own experiences of leveraging his position to advocate for community needs and systemic reform.
# Moral Mondays, Black Lives Matter, and Activism

> Bishop Selders recounts the origins and evolution of the Moral Mondays movement in Connecticut, rooted in his and his wife's involvement in the Ferguson protests and the rise of Black Lives Matter. He describes the integration of faith-based activism with broader social justice issues, the coalition-building across religious and secular lines, and the movement's impact on policy and electoral reform. The chapter concludes with reflections on ongoing efforts to create meaningful change and the importance of sustained activism.

story.title: Dr Vernal and Bishop Selders Oral history
story.description: The interview explores Bishop John Selders' background, emphasizing his deep roots in the Pentecostal church and his pastoral journey, which led him to Hartford, Connecticut. With a rich family history of ecclesiastical service and social activism, Bishop Selders reflects on his cultural identity and experiences in different American regions. He discusses community engagement, particularly the racial and social dynamics in New England, and addresses challenges like voter apathy and political disconnection. Selders underlines the importance of leadership and activism, sharing insights from his involvement in the Moral Monday movement, aimed at addressing social justice and inequality.
